ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for stir-fried egg noodle with beef; FOODON_00002700 (noodle dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP for stir-fried noodles used as closest parent; no specific code for egg noodle with beef variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific stir-fried noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of stir-fried egg noodle with sliced beef, approximately 350 g, as typically served at a Singapore hawker stall or restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Noodles with Meat (Char Kway Teow / Stir-fried noodle with meat composite) — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo data for stir-fried noodle dishes with meat (fried noodles with beef/meat category) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for egg noodles (cooked) and beef (stir-fried). Per-100g values represent a composite of approximately 55% cooked egg noodles, 25% stir-fried sliced beef, 15% mixed vegetables, and 5% sauce/oil, consistent with typical Singapore stir-fried noodle dish composition.

gi_source.title: Noodles, egg, cooked — Glycemic Index Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 50 assigned based on cooked egg noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Egg noodles have a reported GI of approximately 46–55 depending on cooking method; stir-frying with protein and fat further moderates glycemic response. A GI of 50 is used as a conservative central estimate for this mixed dish. GL per serving calculated as (50 × 61.25 g carbohydrate per 350 g serving) / 100 = 30.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1470 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 1470 mg sodium — about 64% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 50) with significant carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 50 and a glycemic load of approximately 30.6 per serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–200 g) and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and elevated sodium per serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains approximately 5.6 g saturated fat and 1470 mg sodium per standard serving; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean beef cuts, request less oil in preparation, and limit frequency of consumption to support lipid and blood pressure management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Egg Noodle with Sliced Beef (牛肉炒鸡蛋面) is a popular Chinese-influenced noodle dish widely served in Singapore's hawker centres, coffee shops, and casual restaurants. The dish features pre-cooked egg noodles — made from wheat flour and egg, giving them a characteristic yellow colour and springy, slightly chewy texture — wok-tossed over high heat with thinly sliced beef, mixed vegetables such as bean sprouts, choy sum, or cabbage, and a savoury sauce typically comprising soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and garlic. The high-heat wok technique imparts a distinctive smoky 'wok hei' flavour. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 148 kcal, 8.5 g protein, 5.2 g fat, and 17.5 g carbohydrate, making it a moderate-energy, protein-containing staple. The glycemic index is estimated at 50 (low GI), though the glycemic load per standard 350 g serving is approximately 30.6, which is clinically significant for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) due to soy and oyster sauce, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or shellfish allergies. It is commonly consumed as a main meal at lunch or dinner and is a staple of Singapore's Chinese-Singaporean culinary tradition.

confidence_reason: No exact match for this specific dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB stir-fried noodle with meat category data and USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked egg noodles and stir-fried beef, combined using a defensible compositional estimate. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database data for cooked egg noodles. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB or USDA SR Legacy source data for this dish category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data for this composite dish. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source data for this dish category. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate based on cooked egg noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; the actual GI of the complete dish may vary depending on beef quantity, sauce composition, and cooking method. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (50 × 61.25) / 100 = 30.6, where carbohydrate per serving = 17.5 g per 100 g × 350 g serving = 61.25 g. Halal status is marked false as the restaurant source (Viet'Spice) is a Vietnamese restaurant and halal certification cannot be confirmed from available data. The dish name in the input includes a menu prefix 'N2.' which has been removed from the canonical food_name per Veritas naming rules.

Constructed composite nutrient values from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA SR Legacy approved sources.
Assigned GI 50 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for cooked egg noodles.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) in notes per G3 requirement.
Verified all aliases_by_language entries appear verbatim in flat aliases list per Q8 consistency rule.
Set ontology match_type values exclusively to approved values: 'closest_parent' and 'none'.
Calculated glycemic_load_per_serving as (50 × 61.25) / 100 = 30.6 for 350 g serving.
